summary: Canadian conceptual photographer (born 1946) known for large-format, staged 'near documentary' photographs, often displayed as backlit transparencies. Known for recreating famous artworks as photographs: Picture for Women (after Manet's A Bar at the Folies-Bergère), The Destroyed Room (after Delacroix's The Death of Sardanapalus), and A Sudden Gust of Wind (after Hokusai). Also known for Mimic (depicting a racist white man mocking an Asian man) and Dead Troops Talk (sold for $3.7 million in 2012). Part of the Vancouver photoconceptualist school.

works.name: Picture for Women (1979)
works.description: Large-format photograph recreating Manet's A Bar at the Folies-Bergère. Shows a camera on a tripod in the center of the frame between a male photographer (Wall himself) in profile and a female model, all reflected in a mirror. Two metal stands split the frame into three sections, and the woman on the left rests her hands on a table. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Recreates Manet's A Bar at the Folies-Bergère; camera on tripod faces a mirror, placed between the male photographer and a female model; two metal stands split the frame into three sections
examples: That work, Picture for Women, is a recreation of A Bar at the Folies-Bergere.
Jeff Wall's Picture for Women was chiefly inspired by a painting by this artist in which a bartender faces the viewers and places her hands next to a dish of oranges.

works.name: The Destroyed Room (1978)
works.description: Photograph inspired by Delacroix's The Death of Sardanapalus. Shows a woman's destroyed room: insulation visible through a torn red wall, broken furniture, a slashed mattress, high-heels and clothes strewn across the floor, and drawers pulled out from a dresser. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Inspired by Delacroix's The Death of Sardanapalus; shows a destroyed room with insulation visible through a torn red wall, broken furniture, slashed mattress, clothes and high-heels strewn about
examples: High-heels and clothes are strewn across the floor in a work by this photographer inspired by The Death of Sardanapalus, in which insulation is visible through a torn red wall behind broken furniture and a slashed mattress.
This photographer channeled... Delacroix's The Destruction of Sardanapalus in his The Destroyed Room.

works.name: A Sudden Gust of Wind (after Hokusai) (1993)
works.description: Photograph recreating Hokusai's 'Ejiri in Suruga Province' print from the Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji series. Shows men in business suits with paper scattered into the air by an airstream (a 'non-accidental accident'). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Recreates a Hokusai print (from Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji); shows men in business suits with paper thrown into an airstream
examples: This photographer of The Destroyed Room portrayed a non-accidental accident by having paper thrown into an airstream in his A Sudden Gust of Wind, which recreates a Hokusai work.
He also recreated Hokusai's A Sudden Gust of Wind using men in business suits.

works.name: Mimic (1982)
works.description: Photograph showing a racist white man pulling the corner of his eye (slanting gesture) as he walks past an Asian man on the street. Part of the Vancouver School of photoconceptualism. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Shows a White man using his finger to mock an Asian man by slanting his eye as he walks past him on the street
examples: A racist white man slants his eye as he walks past an Asian man on the street in this photographer's Mimic.
This conceptualist photographer, who depicted a White man using his finger to mock an Asian man while walking down a street in his Mimic.

works.name: Dead Troops Talk (1992)
works.description: Staged photograph showing several men lying wounded in a trench (set during the Soviet-Afghan War). Sold for $3.7 million in 2012, making it the third most expensive photograph ever sold at that time. Extensively analyzed in the last essay of Susan Sontag's Regarding the Pain of Others. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Depicts several wounded men in a trench; sold for $3.7 million in 2012 (then the third most expensive photograph ever sold)
examples: This man's image of several men lying wounded in a trench entitled Dead Troops Talk sold for $3.7 million in 2012, making it the third most expensive photograph ever sold.

clues.clue: Extensively analyzed in the last essay of Susan Sontag's Regarding the Pain of Others; shows a man dangling flesh in front of another bloody-faced man
examples: A staged photo of a fictional event in this country [Afghanistan], which is extensively analyzed in the last essay of Susan Sontag's Regarding the Pain of Others, depicts a man who dangles a bit of flesh in front of a second, bloody-faced man jokingly riding a third amidst a 'dialogue of the dead.'

works.name: Other Works and General / Biographical
works.description: Canadian photographer born in Vancouver (1946), part of the Vancouver photoconceptualist school. Known for large-format staged 'near documentary' photographs. Also created Restoration (a 180-degree photo of workers mending a panoramic painting); two photos showing the same scene with an octopus vs. a pile of beans on a table; and a photo documenting a man cleaning the Barcelona Pavilion. Known for displaying work as large-scale backlit transparencies. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Canadian conceptual photographer known for staged 'near documentary' photographs; part of the Vancouver photoconceptualist school
examples: For 10 points, name this Canadian conceptual artist known for his staged photographs.
Kevin Lum and Rodney Graham are part of this city's [Vancouver's] photoconceptualist School, as is a photographer who showed a White man pulling at the corner of his eye.

clues.clue: Restoration: a 180-degree photo of workers mending a panoramic painting
examples: This Canadian photographer took a 180 degree photo of workers mending a panoramic painting in his photo Restoration.

clues.clue: Two identical photographs differ only in that one has an octopus and the other has a pile of beans on the left table
examples: Two photographs taken by this artist are identical except that in one photo, an octopus sits on the table on the left and in the other, a pile of beans sit on the table on the right.

comprehensive_summary:
Jeff Wall (born 1946) is a Canadian conceptual photographer from Vancouver, known for large-format staged photographs displayed as backlit transparencies. He is part of the Vancouver photoconceptualist school alongside Kevin Lum and Rodney Graham. Wall frequently recreates well-known artworks as photographs: Picture for Women (1979) recreates Manet's A Bar at the Folies-Bergère, placing Wall's camera on a tripod between himself and a female model, both reflected in a mirror; The Destroyed Room (1978) recreates Delacroix's The Death of Sardanapalus as a wrecked woman's room with torn red walls exposing insulation, broken furniture, a slashed mattress, and clothes strewn on the floor; and A Sudden Gust of Wind (1993) recreates a Hokusai print from Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji with men in business suits and paper thrown into an airstream.

Wall's Mimic (1982) shows a White man pulling at the corner of his eye to mock an Asian man walking past him on the street. Dead Troops Talk (1992) is a staged image of wounded soldiers in a trench set during the Soviet-Afghan War; it sold for $3.7 million in 2012 (then the third most expensive photograph ever sold) and is extensively analyzed in the final essay of Susan Sontag's Regarding the Pain of Others. Wall also made Restoration (a 180-degree panoramic photo of workers mending a large painting) and spent several days photographing a man cleaning the Barcelona Pavilion. Two of his photographs are identical except that one shows an octopus and the other a pile of beans on a table.
recursive_suggestions: A Bar at the Folies-Bergère — Manet painting; Wall's Picture for Women is repeatedly used as a mention clue for this painting
The Death of Sardanapalus — Delacroix painting; Wall's Destroyed Room is used as a mention clue for it
Hokusai — Wall's A Sudden Gust of Wind is repeatedly used as a mention clue for Hokusai questions
